content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 307 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1160 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance          Administration               Executive/Legal               Enforcement               Conflict of Interest
(916) 127-3652              (213) 5660                   (916) 2501                   (213) 6441                (916) 6444


August 11, 1979


                                                                          479-008

John M. Filice, Jr.
Attorney at Law
7951-A Wren Avenue
Gilroy, CA 95020

                                          Re: Opinion Request
                                               No. 79-008

Dear Mr. Filice:

     Thank you for your letter of July 19, 1979.  Because
the questions you have asked do not present a substantial
issue of interpretation of the Political Reform Act, your
request for a formal opinion of the Commission has been
denied. However, I hope the following informal advice pro-
vided pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b)1/ proves
helpful to you.

     The facts, as I understand them, are as follows:  The
Gilroy municipal elections are scheduled for November 1979.
In that election four city council seats, including that of
the mayor, will be voted upon.  A group of property owners
and developers have, from time to time, informally discussed
the political climate of the city and whether responsible
industrial, commercial and residential growth would be sup-
ported by the community.

     Five members of the discussion group and an incumbent
councilman have now paid for a survey to poll citizens on
issues such as crime and drug dealing at Gilroy High School,
industrial and residential growth in the city and construction
of a new city hall.  The survey also seeks opinions concern-
ing the performance of four incumbent city councilmembers,
including the mayor and the name recognition profile of
three potential city council candidates.  Lastly, the survey
contains a straw poll with respect to three potential mayoral
candidates.


_________________________________
1/  All statutory references are to the Government Code.

John M. Fillce, Jr.
August 13, 1979
Page Two


     The incumbent councilmember who paid part of the cost
of the survey is not a member of the group that has informally met to discuss community issues. The councilmember
paid part of the costs of the survey because he has an interest in running for mayor and believed that combining
efforts would lower the survey cost. The group made clear
to councilmember that his participation in the survey
did not constitute the group's endorsement.

     You have asked whether the survey will trigger any
reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act of
1974. We have concluded that the discussion group is a
recipient committee under the terms of Section 82013(a) and
that the councilmember is a candidate under the terms of
Section 82007.

     To qualify as a committee, a group of organization must
receive "contributions" or make "expenditures" over a specified threshold level. Section 82013. For the purpose of
this section, only those payments which are received or made
for a political purpose are contributions or expenditures.
Sections 82015, 82025. Political purpose is defined in part
as influencing or attempting to influence the action of the
voters for or against a candidate or measure. Section 82013,
2 Cal. Adm. Code sections 18215, 18225.

     The fact that there is an upcoming election in Gilroy,
the fact the survey seeks opinions concerning candidates in
that election and the nature of the group contracting for
the survey suggest that the purpose of the survey is to
determine strategy for the election with respect to candidates to be supported and opposed by members of the group
participating in the survey. These circumstances remove
this survey from the type of issue discussion which campaign
disclosure cannot reach. See Buckley v. Valeo, 424 U.S. 1
(1976). Therefore, the survey activity must be deemed to be
carried out for the purpose of influencing or attempting to
influence the November municipal elections.

     Because the survey activity has the requisite "political
purpose," payments made and received in connection with the
survey are deemed contributions and expenditures. Since the
group conducting the survey will have received in excess of
$500 in contributions, it has qualified as a recipient committee. As a recipient committee, the group is required to
file a statement of organization and semiannual statements
reporting its contributions and expenditures. Sections
84101, 84200, 84206.

John M. Filice, Jr.
August 13, 1979
Page Three

Section 4200 defined a candidate as one who "makes an
expenditure ... with a view towards bringing about his nomi-
nation or election to any elective office...." When a person
conducts a survey to determine his prospects in a future
election, he clearly is doing so with a view towards bringing
about his election. Thus, the councilmember is a candidate
and must file campaign statements reporting the survey
expenditure.2/

Although the payments made and received in connection
with the survey are contributions and expenditures, the
group and its members who have paid for the survey cannot be
considered to have made a contribution to the councilmember.
As the facts indicate, the councilmember's participation in
the survey was based upon a desire to lower costs and not to
support or bestow any benefit upon the councilmember. In
these circumstances, we cannot characterize any benefit the
councilmember may receive from the survey as a contribution
by the other survey contributors or the group itself.

If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request,
you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.
If you have any further questions, please call me or Lee
Rosenthal at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Michael Bennett
Executive Director

LCRLAP

_______________

2/   Apart from the councilmember's status as a candi-
date, he would have an obligation to report the survey expense.
Elected officeholders are required to file campaign statements
even when they are not candidates. Section 84206. Those
statements must disclose any payment he has made unless it
is clear from surrounding circumstances that the payment was
made for personal purposes unrelated to his candidacy or
status as an officeholder. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18223(b)(1).
Since the councilmember's survey payment clearly was not made
for personal purposes unrelated to his candidacy or status
as an officeholder, it is a reportable expenditure.

MILLER, PERRIN, DOMINO,
GIACALONE & ACKERMAN
1611 THE ALAMEDA
SAN JOSE, CALIFORNIA 95126
408 287-5712

JOHN M. FILICE, JR.
ATTORNEY AT LAW
7951-A WREN AVENUE
GILROY, CALIFORNIA 95020
408 847-2110

79 008
Request No. ________

Date Received 7/23/79

Response Due 8/2/79

PROPOSED RESPONSE
DUE: 7/27/79

July 19, 1979

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES
COMMISSION
PO Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Gentlemen:

Approximately a month ago I contacted Mr. John Giansello of
your office relative to a question dealing with the Political
Reform Act which will be set forth below. After I stated the
facts of the question to Mr. Giansello, he indicated that he did
not feel that the conduct was a reportable act or that our group
constituted a committee under the Political Reform Act. Mr. Giansello advised that he would like to take up the matter with members
of staff and that he would contact me.

Subsequently, Mr. Giansello telephoned and advised that he
could not add anything to his original opinion other than the
fact that there seemed to be a disagreement among the staff. He
indicated that he would contact me further on this matter. Recently, my office was contacted and since I was not present, my
associate, Fred Domino, took the call and was advised that the
staff now felt that the conduct was reportable and that the group
should file as a committee.

Because my inquiry apparently involves a grey area, I respectfully request that the Commission issue an opinion with
respect to our duties under the Act.

The City of Gilroy will be holding its municipal elections on
the first Tuesday of November, 1979. Four (4) City Council seats
will be up for election, including the mayor's seat. Also, in
November, 1978, a controversial item was placed on the Gilroy ballot pertaining to an advisory vote of the citizens as to whether
they favored or opposed a residential project in the vicinity of
the existing municipal golf course.

From time to time, a group of property owners and developers
have informally discussed the political climate of the City as to
whether reasonable industrial, commercial and residential growth
would be supported by the community. Obviously, there is also
considerable interest in the upcoming elections. After the two-to-
one defeat of the golf course/residential project, and the preparation of an extensive growth management ordinance, many property
owners and developers became concerned that the City would soon
adopt a no growth posture. It has been often suggested by various
members of the group that a survey would be helpful in determining

iar Political Practices
Commission
Page 2


whether building projects would receive the support of the com-
munity.

One of the members of the City Council who has an office
adjoining mine has expressed interest in running for mayor. He
*informed me that he had been in contact with a company that
conducts surveys and that he was interested in determining his
popularity. I informed him that I and some other people were
likewise interested in doing a survey and that possibly we could
combine our efforts in order to lower the cost. It was estimated
that a contribution of $500.00 from each participant would pay for
the survey.

I and the group made it clear to the Councilman and the sur-
vey company that we were not endorsing him for any office and that
the survey was being conducted for each person's internal use. At-
tached hereto is a copy of the questions asked in the survey. As
the enclosure indicates, the questions cover a broad range of com-
munity interest. Six individuals and firms (including the Council-
man) paid for the survey. To date, the results of the survey have
been retained by the participating individuals and to my knowledge
not released to any other persons or firms.

It is my opinion that the above described conduct does not
fall within the purview of the Political Reform Act of 1974 as
amended. Specifically, I do not believe that a general survey
by itself can be construed as an act "attempting to influence
the action of the voters for or against the nomination or elec-
tion of one or more candidates or the passage or defeat of any
measure . . ." Regarding the "contribution aspect", the poten-
tial candidate paid for his fair share of the survey and in
accordance with the Administrative Code's definition of "political
purposes" it would again seem that the Act does not apply. It
might be noted, that a survey could have been conducted by the
potential candidate relative to his popularity, vis-à-vis the
existing mayor and other councilmen, and it is probable that
his costs would not have exceed the $500.00 payment.

If the theory of the staff is that the potential candidate
received an indirect benefit relative to the survey; query, if
the results are released to all potential candidates or the
public, does this action change the circumstances?

I again reiterate that we do not believe that the act of
taking a survey can be construed to be an attempt to influence
the action of the voters, however, we would obviously follow
the Commission's recommendation. I again state that the group
has never expressed any intention one way or the other toward
any individual's candidacy and the intent was to gather general
community information that would be helpful regarding future
decisions.

Fair Political Practices
Commission
Page 3


I would appreciate it if you would contact me as soon as
possible as to whether an opinion will be issued.

Very truly yours,

JOHN W. PILICE, JR.

JMF:mE


cc: Mr. John Giansello
    Mr. Lee Rosenthal

1. What do you think is the most important problem facing Gilroy?

2. (Second most important problem?)

How would you rate the job being done by:

3. Gilroy Mayor, Norman Goodrich
(Excellent, Good, Only fair, Poor)

4. Police Chief, Jim Laizure (LAY' - zure)

5. The Gilroy City Council

6. City Councilperson, Roberta Hughan (HEM' - in)

7. City Councilperson, David Stout

8. City Councilperson, Brian Cunningham

9. Gilroy School System

10. The Gilroy Industrial Development Committee

11. The Gilroy Chamber of Commerce

12. The Gilroy Police Department

13. Committee for Orderly Growth (COG)

Now I am going to read you a list of names. For each, please tell me if you recognize the name, and tell me what that person does.

14. Cathy Jorgenson. (Owner, employment agency; Chairperson, Y.O.G.)

15. Robert Taylor (Retired Highway Patrolman)

16. Hy Miller (Realtor, member Planning Commission)

Now I am going to read you a list of statements. For each please tell me if you agree strongly, agree, disagree, or disagree strongly with the statement.

17. Because of the rise of crime and drug dealing, Gilroy High School should have a "closed campus"

18. City employees [illegible] right to take [illegible] with them.

19. Gilroy needs more industrial growth to provide [illegible] for our residents.

20. Gilroy needs a new City Hall if it can be built without new city taxes.

21. Gilroy should upgrade procedures to attract new industry.

22. Gilroy needs a youth recreation center.

23. Gilroy already has enough fast food outlets. New fast food operations on First Street should be limited.

24. Gilroy is becoming too much of a "bedroom community" for San Jose. We need more industry to provide jobs on a local level.

25. Do you think there is too much, too little, or just about the right amount of growth in Gilroy?

(Too much, too little, about right amount)

26. What type of growth, if any, do you think Gilroy needs?

(list)

Because of Proposition 13, some cuts will have to be made in Gilroy's city budget. I am going to read you a list of budget categories. For each, please tell me if you think more, less of the same budget monies should be appropriated this year.

27. City Employees' Salaries

28. City Administrators' Salaries

29. Police and Fire [illegible]

30. Parks and Recreation

31. Did you support or oppose last November's golf course issue?

(Support or Oppose)

32. Why did you (support/oppose) it.

33. Do you support or oppose school busing in Gilroy?

(Support or Oppose)

This November, the voters of Gilroy will be electing a mayor. The
following people have been suggested as possible candidates. If an election
were held today, for whom would you vote?

33. Norman Goodrich, Incumbent

34. Roberta Hüghan (NEW-in), City Councilperson

35. Brian Cunningham, City Councilperson

Now, a few questions for statistical purposes only.

36. How long have you lived in Gilroy?

37. Own or Rent?

38. Work in Gilroy?

39. Age

40. Race

41. Income

- Sex

- Party

- Geo Code

sections.question: Whether a survey conducted by a group of property owners and developers (with participation by an incumbent councilman interested in running for mayor) regarding community issues, candidate performance, and potential mayoral candidates triggers reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act of 1974.
sections.conclusion: The discussion group qualifies as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a) because the survey activity has the requisite 'political purpose' of influencing the November municipal election with respect to candidates to be supported or opposed. The incumbent councilmember qualifies as a candidate under Section 82007 and must file campaign statements reporting the survey expenditure. However, other group members cannot be considered to have made a contribution to the councilmember because the councilmember's participation was based on cost reduction rather than support or benefit bestowment. The group must file a statement of organization and campaign statements.
sections.question_synthetic: Does a survey conducted by property owners and developers on community growth issues and candidate performance, partially funded by an incumbent councilman considering a mayoral run, constitute a reportable committee activity and candidate expenditure under the Political Reform Act?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that the survey group constitutes a recipient committee and the councilman is a candidate with reporting obligations for the survey expense, even though other participants did not make a contribution to the councilman. The group must file required campaign disclosure statements because the survey's political purpose of influencing the election exceeded the $500 threshold.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an FPPC response letter (advice letter) denying a formal opinion request but providing informal guidance under Government Code Section 83114(b). The incoming request letter from attorney Filice is included as context. The FPPC response clearly sets forth both the question and conclusion regarding recipient committee and candidate status.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a survey conducted by a group of property owners and developers (with participation by an incumbent councilman interested in running for mayor) regarding community issues, candidate performance, and potential mayoral candidates triggers reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act of 1974.

CONCLUSION: The discussion group qualifies as a recipient committee under Section 82013(a) because the survey activity has the requisite 'political purpose' of influencing the November municipal election with respect to candidates to be supported or opposed. The incumbent councilmember qualifies as a candidate under Section 82007 and must file campaign statements reporting the survey expenditure. However, other group members cannot be considered to have made a contribution to the councilmember because the councilmember's participation was based on cost reduction rather than support or benefit bestowment. The group must file a statement of organization and campaign statements.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provided informal advice denying a formal opinion request but concluding that a survey funded by property owners and an incumbent councilman regarding community growth and candidate performance triggers Political Reform Act reporting obligations. The group qualifies as a recipient committee and the councilman as a candidate with survey expenditure reporting duties.
